title: Opsgenie Zero-Recipient and Heartbeat Lapse — On-Call Routing Gaps
app: Opsgenie Splunk integration, Outgoing Webhook/OEC forwarder
spl:
index=alerts (sourcetype="opsgenie:alert" OR sourcetype="opsgenie:heartbeat")
| eval alert_gap=if(sourcetype="opsgenie:alert" AND (match(lower(message),"(?i)no recipients|empty rotation|no one on-call") OR recipients_count=0 OR mvcount(recipients)=0),1,0)
| eval hb_gap=if(sourcetype="opsgenie:heartbeat" AND status IN ("expired","inactive"),1,0)
| where alert_gap=1 OR hb_gap=1
| stats count, latest(_time) as t, values(team) as teams by integration_name, source
| sort -count
description: Opsgenie alerts that fire with zero recipients or heartbeats that expire without follow-up indicate broken rotations, deleted schedules, or integration misrouting—distinct from PagerDuty schedule exports. Catching these patterns prevents silent failures in hybrid paging estates.
value: Ensures hybrid Opsgenie + PagerDuty environments still have a human on the hook during sev-1 events.
implementation: Normalize `recipients` multivalue field from JSON arrays. Join to `team` roster CSV for expected coverage. Differentiate from UC-16.2.22 (PagerDuty schedule gaps) by focusing on Opsgenie sourcetypes only.
visualization: Timeline of zero-recipient alerts, table (integration × team), map of expired heartbeats by region.
grandmaExplanation: We look for emergency signals that nobody was scheduled to receive, and for heartbeat checks that died quietly. Either situation means an alarm might never reach a person.
